questions.prompt: Defining cost advantage of R2 over AWS S3?

choices.text: Zero egress fees. AWS S3 charges $0.05-0.09 per GB egress (data leaving AWS to users or other clouds); at any scale this dominates costs. A site serving 10TB of images/month pays AWS $500-900 in egress alone. R2 charges $0 for egress — only pay for storage ($0.015/GB) and operations. The cost difference for media-heavy or download-heavy workloads is dramatic. R2 also has slightly cheaper storage per GB. The egress story is what makes R2 a viable replacement for S3 in most consumer-facing use cases; for pure internal storage with no external reads, S3's ecosystem might still win, but external-serving workloads benefit enormously from R2.

questions.explanation: Zero egress fees. AWS charges $0.05-0.09/GB out; R2 is $0. Dominates economics for serving workloads.

questions.prompt: Worker serves user-uploaded images. How to make them fast at edge?

choices.text: Combine R2 + Cache API. Worker checks `caches.default` first; on miss, fetches from R2 and stores in cache with appropriate Cache-Control headers (`public, max-age=86400`); subsequent requests hit edge cache (microseconds) instead of R2 storage. Use `ctx.waitUntil(cache.put(...))` so the cache write doesn't block the response. For dynamic images, layer Cloudflare Image Resizing on top — URL-based transforms (resize, format conversion) with edge caching. R2 alone is fast but adds storage-tier latency; Cache API in front turns repeated reads into edge-cached responses. Standard pattern for any media-heavy Workers app.

questions.explanation: R2 + Cache API: first request fetches + caches; subsequent serves from edge in microseconds.
